title: Context Switch Anomalies Detection
spl:
index=os sourcetype=vmstat host=*
| bin _time span=5m
| stats avg(cs) as cs by host, _time
| eventstats avg(cs) as baseline stdev(cs) as stddev by host
| where cs > baseline + 3*stddev
description: Context switch anomalies indicate scheduler issues or unexpected process workload changes.
value: Context switch anomalies indicate scheduler issues or unexpected process workload changes.
knownFalsePositives: Short time windows after boot, during `fork` storms from CI, or when `vmstat` sampling cadence changes can make `stdev` unstable. Hosts with very low `cs` can have tiny standard deviations so the 3-sigma rule trips on noiseŌĆöset a floor on `stddev` or minimum sample count if needed.
implementation: Use vmstat context switch field (`cs`) with statistical anomaly detection per host. Alert on 3-sigma deviations from the rolling baseline. Correlate with `ps`/`top` or service restarts for cause.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
ŌĆó Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_nix`.
ŌĆó Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=vmstat`.
ŌĆó For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ŌĆö Configure data collection
Use vmstat context switch field with statistical anomaly detection. Alert on 3-sigma deviations from baseline. Include process state analysis (`top`, `pidstat`, `ps`) on Linux to identify cause.

Step 2 ŌĆö Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=os sourcetype=vmstat host=*
| bin _time span=5m
| stats avg(cs) as cs by host, _time
| eventstats avg(cs) as baseline stdev(cs) as stddev by host
| where cs > baseline + 3*stddev
```

Understanding this SPL

**Context Switch Anomalies Detection** ŌĆö Context switch anomalies indicate scheduler issues or unexpected process workload changes.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=vmstat`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_nix`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedŌĆörename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: os; **sourcetype**: vmstat. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

ŌĆó Scopes the data: index=os, sourcetype=vmstat. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
ŌĆó Buckets time into 5-minute bins, then averages **cs** (context switches per interval) per host and bucket.
ŌĆó `eventstats` computes per-host mean and standard deviation of those bucketed averages so each row compares to its own recent history.
ŌĆó Filters rows where the current bucket is more than three standard deviations above the meanŌĆötune span and sigma as needed.

CIM does not expose raw `vmstat` context switches; keep this search on `sourcetype=vmstat`.


Step 3 ŌĆö Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ŌĆö Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Timechart, Anomaly Detector
grandmaExplanation: We watch how often the Linux kernel switches between processes so we can catch surprising churn that usually means something is thrashing or stuck in a loop.
